steps.text: Determine whether the scope of experiments conducted at the facility were consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.
Determine whether the licensee’s procedures for the control and conduct of experiments are consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.

Determine whether the licensee reviewed and approved experiments performed at the facility, and any subsequent changes made to the experiments, in accordance with Technical Specification (TS) requirements and the licensee’s procedural requirements.
Determine whether the licensee reviewed experiments for TS changes or compliance to 10 CFR 50.59.
Determine whether the licensee identified hazards associated with the experiments conducted since the last inspection and took appropriate remedial action.
Determine whether the licensee predicted the approximate reactivity effect on the reactor before conducting the experiment.
Determine whether the licensee confirmed the reactivity effect as required by the TS.

Determine whether the licensee accounted for irradiated items until disposing of them or until they decayed to an acceptable level, in accordance with regulatory requirements and applicable licensee procedures.
Determine whether the licensee’s experiments are constrained as required by the TS.
Determine whether researchers adhered to the licensees procedural controls when loading experiments into the test facilities and removing them from the test facilities.
Determine whether the experiments reduced the operator’s ability to control reactivity manipulations or the protective system’s ability to shut down the reactor if needed.

constraints.text: The licensee's experiments are constrained as required by the TS.

steps.text: Determine whether engineered radiation safety controls were used during experiments and posting was accomplished as required.
Review documentation available at the office prior to the onsite inspection.
Review the licensee’s procedures for the control and conduct of experiments prior to the onsite inspection.
Directly observe 50 percent of the new experiments or changes to experiments since the last inspection.
Review records and discuss with cognizant personnel if direct observation is not practical.
Review information available that authorizes the conduct of a particular experiment.
Submit a proposal with details of the experiment to the safety review committee for Class 2, 3, and 4 experiments.

constraints.text: Particular attention is to be given to experiments that contain special nuclear material or explosives.

steps.text: Approve routine experiments such as gold foil irradiation by the reactor supervisor, a licensed senior reactor operator (SRO), or the radiation control officer.
Document routine experiments for each new group of experimenters, or whenever the experiment has not been performed for one calendar year or more by the original experimenter.
Verify that routine experiments pose no hazard to the reactor, the personnel, or the public.
Approve experiments that could pose a hazard by the safety review committee and verify conditions are met before conducting.
Approve experiments with significant potential hazards by the safety review committee.
Insert, disassemble, or remove experiments from the reactor under the direct supervision of the reactor supervisor or a duly authorized SRO.
Obtain written description of reactor operations approved by the safety review committee before inserting an experiment.

constraints.text: Approval of experiments that could pose a hazard to the reactor, personnel, or the public.
Require NRC review and approval for the use of explosives within a reactor facility.
Operators and experimenters must understand the hazards associated with an experiment.
Opening the experiment tube could be radiologically hazardous or cause a reactivity transient.
If a regulating rod is changed by that amount, the operator will stop the experiment and calculate the value to assure compliance with constraint limits and shutdown margin limits.
The need for constraints will depend on the type of reactor, and will be specifically stated in the TS.
An experiment can be considered constrained if it has a large mass or heavy shielding that minimize potential movement.

Experiments (samples) with a worth of less than 0.002 delta K/K can be inserted or removed with a rabbit system or by hand while the reactor is critical, if the expected radiation field for the personnel does not present an exposure problem.
